title: Injecting the glabella. Injection Points & Safety Advice
filename: Injecting the glabella. Injection Points & Safety Advice.en.vtt
excerpt: Injecting the debella important safety tips and advice on how to get consistent results and not cause side effects this week we're going into all of the detailed questions that you guys have been asking what about the difference between male and female lelas what about injection …

segments.text: injecting the debella important safety tips and advice on how to get consistent results and not cause side effects this week we're going into all of the detailed questions that you guys have been asking what about the difference between male and female lelas what about injection depth how do we avoid the frontalis how do you avoid a droopy eyelid these are all are fundamentals that you must have clear in your mind to inject with confidence so let's get started before we dive in I've created something new that I think many of you going to love if you want to learn systematically cover all the injections processes and all the information that you need to be a topnotch injector of but toxins make sure you click on the link that comes with this video to find out more injection confidence always starts with understanding the anatomy so what's the anatomy of the debella that you must know first of all it's all about the origin and the insertion of the core muscles that we're going to treat the main muscle is the corrugator supercilii this muscle runs medially from the nasan all the way up to the

insertion point just lateral to the mid pupillary line above the eyebrow that whole muscle is pulling inwards and creating your El's line or crease we also have the procerus which pulls the brow down and this muscle has its origin on the nasan and runs up into the dermis of the forehead those two muscles do most of the activity but there's also an interesting muscle sometimes debated called the depressor supercilii and this muscle is often described as part of the vular oculi muscle but sometimes also seen as a separate muscle the truth is for injections it doesn't actually matter because it overlaps so closely with exactly where you'd want to inject the corrugator supercilii now did you notice how those muscles all changed plain in terms of how I described them starting on the bone and going into the dermis this is a crucial piece of information for injecting accurately we should do the same with our injections where we think the muscle is intermediate we inject at an intermediate depth where we think it's superficial we inject eject superficially this also improves your

safety let's go on to that as our next topic which is how to avoid complications so what are the complications that you might get from treating the debella the most important one would be the eyelid if you affect the levator ply muscle within the eyelid you are likely to get a droopy eyelid this only happens when you inject deep enough for The Botch liim toxin to make its way into the orbit for botch toxin to make its way into the orbit we have to have injected deeply the orbit is shielded by multiple structures it shielded by the orbital membrane there's precept or fat pads there are the muscles themselves which actually provide somewhat of a shield so the depth of injection is key if you know your anatomy and you're injecting at the right depth it should be very rare to get a droopy eyelid the next common complication would be around treating the frontalis muscle by mistake so the corrugator and the frontalis muscle are actually intertwined if you look at the anatomy of the corrugator muscle as it becomes more superficial it actually passes through the frontalis muscle and that means at the point of insertion which you can actually usually

see when someone frowns above that muscle is frontalis and lateral to the muscle is frontalis if you get boty toxin into the frontalis at these crucial points you likely to get an asymmetrical eyebrow so maybe one side will lift and the other will not or even worse a droopy eyebrow where the eyebrow slumps down and causes an asymmetry at rest or a tired appearance which can either be unilateral or bilateral depending on where the frontalis was affected now that's same issue with the corrugator muscle where it inserts is the most sensitive area for a misplaced injection but actually all the way along the path of the corrugator muscle you can still affect the frontalis muscle which is above it and this strangely happens to extremely cautious injectors more than it happens to others and there's a reason for this sometimes when new and very cautious people start injecting they want to leave a little bit of extra safety margin compared with what they were taught they're very afraid of being near the eye so they move a little bit superiorly only 5 mm or so but this usually means you completely miss the corrugator muscle

and directly inject the frontalis muscle and this causes a medial brow drop often with a lateral brow lift which is your Spock brow these are the core complications that you might see and should be able to avoid now with Bim toxin when treating the gabella so why would a patient request treatment in the gabella the most common reason is that they look cross when they're not so this could be a crease at rest sometimes called the eles but also can be that they are a habitual frowner so someone who frowns all the time whether they're Cross or not and these people are often younger who just happen to have that expression now that expression creates a negative Aura on the face it makes them look less approachable and this makes them want to seek treatment so that they look more happy uh and therefore able to collaborate more with others this is not how they request the treatment but that's the underlying driver people want to connect with other people and a constant frown can stop you from connecting so what are the typical in ction points you'd use when treating the debellum there are a number of different ways of doing this and I'll start with the licensed dose is

usually a spread of equally sized injections of four units that's for most of the products that are similar to the alagan type nomenclature so four units times five injection points there's one in the procerus and two into each corrugator now this is a very effective way of completely neutralizing little Bella but you can often get a very similar result for some people a better result leaving a little bit of movement with fewer injections so the next most common pattern I would use involves three points with this style of injection we inject the breras and one injection either side usually also four units but sometimes a bit more and this leaves a little bit of lateral movement which can be a good thing because it looks more natural and of course it does mean you need less product to create a good result another variation that I use for patients who really want a still result with no movement is to separate the final 4unit injection into a three and a one unit injection so this means you're injecting the procerus with four units the bulk of the corrugator with four units then a 3unit injection

at an intermediate point and then one unit right into the insertion point or just before the insertion point of the muscle and this means that your patient does not come back for the followup if you haven't noticed this already many patients will come back with a little follow-up request when they have a small twitch at the side and that's quite a lot of time and effort for something you could have done in the first treatment and that's why I put my one unit just near that insertion Point what about differences between male and female patients now there's a lot of crossover where they are actually exactly the same in the debella but on the extremes you tend to have much smaller and weaker gellas in females and stronger more muscular gellas in males I think there's also a difference often in the direction of pull with LaBella and you'll notice that many females are a little bit more neutral with the angle of the corrugator whereas more males will pull heavy and down it may just be to do with the strength of the muscle but overall the biggest difference from a clinical perspective is the dose of the bot toxin you're likely to use there are very few females who aren't adequately treated with a 20 unit dose but you will meet a fair number of men

who need 30 40 units even to completely make their muscles still and this is particularly true if they are very muscular men who work out in gyms that activity the growth hormone associated with those sorts of activities means that you have bulkier muscles that need higher doses the way around this in your practice if you're not charging by the unit is to Simply have a rule like in the UK we often treat by areas just include in your small print that the areas are for up to 20 units and then if you go over that becomes a second area and that protects you a little bit because I know many of you are treating men who have much bigger muscles and charging them the same as women and actually making a loss uh in your practice so how can we save a little bit of toxin and still get happy patients this is your bonus from me this week most important thing is to be accurate with your injection so if you get your B crime toxin right near where those nerve receptors are you're going to get a slightly better bang for your buck than if they're a bit further away so think a lot about your Anatomy the middle of the

muscle is usually a better place to treat than the periphery so if you focus on the belly of the muscles that you're trying to treat you'll get a better result so that's where our bulk of the toxin goes when treating the gabella is in this injection here it's also helpful to allow your patients to see the benefit of some movement I personally like my glabella to work a little bit because otherwise I feel like I can't convey certain points I want to emphasize in quite the same way but I don't like developing the crease so for me an intermediate amount of movement is perfect and the three-point injection makes me happier than a five-point injection and you could have a certain coort of patients who feel exactly the same way who prefer lower doses so have the discussion you don't have to freeze every muscle on everyone's face in order to have a very happy group of patients in fact probably you'll have happier patients if you do it that way [Music]
